content.content:
160	/// Member selection matches `tsc`'s order-dependent rule: `tsc` (and tsz) pick
161	/// the **first failing member in source order** and elaborate *that* member's
162	/// position/leaf. The forward union (`[number, number] | [string, string]`)
163	/// fails on its first member at position 0 (`number` -> `string`); the reversed
164	/// union (`[string, string] | [number, number]`) fails on *its* first member at
165	/// position 1 (`string` -> `number`). Asserting the concrete member type,
166	/// position, and leaf relation per ordering proves the *right* member is
167	/// selected (not merely that both chains share the TS2322/TS2626 shape, which
168	/// would not catch a contradictory member pick).
169	#[test]
170	fn union_member_selection_matches_tsc_first_failing_member_per_order() {
171	    let forward = ts2322_chain(&diagnostics(
172	        r#"
173	type Target = [id: string, count: number];
174	type Source = [id: number, count: number] | [id: string, count: string];
175	declare const s: Source;
176	const t: Target = s;
177	"#,
178	    ));
179	    // forward: first member `[id: number, count: number]`, position 0, number -> string
180	    assert!(
181	        chain_has(&forward, 0, |m| m.contains("number")) // member header names the number-keyed member
182	            && chain_has(&forward, 1, |m| m.contains("position 0"))
183	            && chain_has(&forward, 2, |m| m.contains("'number'") && m.contains("'string'")),
184	        "forward union should select its first failing member at position 0 \
185	         (number -> string); got {forward:?}"
186	    );
187	
188	    let reversed = ts2322_chain(&diagnostics(
189	        r#"
190	type Target = [id: string, count: number];
191	type Source = [id: string, count: string] | [id: number, count: number];
192	declare const s: Source;
193	const t: Target = s;
194	"#,
195	    ));
196	    // reversed: first member `[id: string, count: string]`, position 1, string -> number
197	    assert!(
198	        chain_has(&reversed, 1, |m| m.contains("position 1"))
199	            && chain_has(&reversed, 2, |m| m.contains("'string'")
200	                && m.contains("'number'")),
201	        "reversed union should select its first failing member at position 1 \
202	         (string -> number); got {reversed:?}"
203	    );
204	}
205	
206	/// Nullish member priority matches `tsc`'s relation order. `tsc`'s
207	/// `eachTypeRelatedToType` walks the source union in ascending *type-id* order,
208	/// where the intrinsic `undefined` and `null` types (allocated before any user
209	/// type) sort first — so when a nullish member and a non-nullish member both
210	/// fail, `tsc` elaborates the *nullish* one, regardless of where it appears in
211	/// the union's *printed* order (which keeps `undefined`/`null` last). tsz stores
212	/// union members in that printer order, so without restoring the relation order
213	/// it would elaborate the non-nullish member (`number`) instead of the nullish
214	/// one (`undefined`/`null`).
215	#[test]
216	fn union_member_selection_prioritizes_nullish_like_tsc() {
217	    // `number | undefined` -> `string`: both members fail; tsc reports `undefined`.
218	    let undef = ts2322_chain(&diagnostics(
219	        r#"
220	declare const x: number | undefined;
221	const y: string = x;
222	"#,
223	    ));
224	    assert!(
225	        chain_has_leaf(&undef, "undefined", "string"),
226	        "expected the `undefined` member to be elaborated; got {undef:?}"
227	    );
228	    assert!(
229	        !chain_has_leaf(&undef, "number", "string"),
230	        "the non-nullish `number` member must not be the elaborated member \
231	         when `undefined` is present; got {undef:?}"
232	    );
233	
234	    // Reversed print/source order must not change the pick — still `undefined`.
235	    let undef_rev = ts2322_chain(&diagnostics(
236	        r#"
237	declare const x: undefined | number;
238	const y: string = x;
239	"#,

content.content:
1150	        }
1151	
1152	        if let (Some(lit), Some(t_kind)) = (
1153	            literal_value(self.interner, source),
1154	            intrinsic_kind(self.interner, target),
1155	        ) {
1156	            let compatible = match lit {
1157	                LiteralValue::String(_) => t_kind == IntrinsicKind::String,
1158	                LiteralValue::Number(_) => t_kind == IntrinsicKind::Number,
1159	                LiteralValue::BigInt(_) => t_kind == IntrinsicKind::Bigint,
1160	                LiteralValue::Boolean(_) => t_kind == IntrinsicKind::Boolean,
1161	            };
1162	            if !compatible {
1163	                return Some(SubtypeFailureReason::LiteralTypeMismatch {
1164	                    source_type: source,
1165	                    target_type: target,
1166	                });
1167	            }
1168	            return None;
1169	        }
1170	
1171	        if intrinsic_kind(self.interner, source).is_some()
1172	            && literal_value(self.interner, target).is_some()
1173	        {
1174	            return Some(SubtypeFailureReason::TypeMismatch {
1175	                source_type: source,
1176	                target_type: target,
1177	            });
1178	        }
1179	
1180	        // Union source: the relation failed, so at least one member is not
1181	        // assignable to the target. tsc keeps the root mismatch visible by
1182	        // elaborating the first failing member beneath the union-to-target line
1183	        // (`Type 'A | B' is not assignable to type 'T'.` -> `Type 'B' is not
1184	        // assignable to type 'T'.`). Without this, the chain stops at the bare
1185	        // union line and hides why the assignment fails (e.g. the `undefined`
1186	        // member contributed by an optional property).
1187	        //
1188	        // Prefer the evaluated `resolved_source` when it is still a union (e.g. a
1189	        // conditional/mapped type that evaluates *into* a union). Otherwise fall
1190	        // back to `pre_eval_source`: when subtype reduction during `evaluate_type`
1191	        // collapsed a written union of structurally-related members (e.g.
1192	        // `string[] | [string, string]` -> `string[]`), the member elaboration
1193	        // must still walk the members tsc preserves under `UnionReduction.Literal`.
1194	        let (union_member_source, union_member_list) =
1195	            match union_list_id(self.interner, resolved_source) {
1196	                Some(list) => (resolved_source, Some(list)),
1197	                None => (
1198	                    pre_eval_source,
1199	                    union_list_id(self.interner, pre_eval_source),
1200	                ),
1201	            };
1202	        if let Some(member_list) = union_member_list {
1203	            let members = self.interner.type_list(member_list);
1204	            for &member in members.iter() {
1205	                if member == source || member == union_member_source {
1206	                    // Defensive: avoid self-recursion on a degenerate union.
1207	                    continue;
1208	                }
1209	                if self.check_subtype(member, target).is_true() {
1210	                    continue;
1211	                }
1212	                // Elaborate the first failing member beneath the union-to-target
1213	                // line, mirroring tsc which always drills into that member. Each
1214	                // arm below is a member-failure reason whose render composes
1215	                // under the union line:
1216	                //   * leaf relations, property summaries
1217	                //     (`MissingProperty`/`MissingProperties`), and the
1218	                //     array-element and readonly-to-mutable reasons self-head —
1219	                //     their rendered line already names the member
1220	                //     (`Property 'a' is missing in type '{ b: 2; }' …`,
1221	                //     `Type 'number[]' is not assignable to type 'string[]' …`,
1222	                //     `The type 'readonly [number]' is 'readonly' …`).
1223	                //   * the tuple/property element-type, index-signature, and
1224	                //     function-return mismatches are header-led; the union-source
1225	                //     renderer supplies the `Type 'M' is not assignable to type
1226	                //     'T'.` member header before drilling (`Type at position 0 …`
1227	                //     / `Types of property 'p' …` / `'string' index signatures are
1228	                //     incompatible.` / the bare return-relation leaf).
1229	                //   * `ParameterTypeMismatch` self-heads with the signature
1230	                //     relation line at depth >= 1, so the renderer routes it
1231	                //     through the self-heading path (its own first line doubles as
1232	                //     the member header), then drills `Types of parameters 'a' and
1233	                //     'b' are incompatible.` + the contravariant leaf.
1234	                // Without this the chain collapses to the bare union-to-target
1235	                // line and hides which member fails.
1236	                //
1237	                // The set is intentionally limited to member-failure shapes whose
1238	                // render composes exactly with `tsc` here.
1239	                //
1240	                // Tuple fixed-arity (`TupleElementMismatch`) and variadic-arity
1241	                // (`TupleArityMismatch`) count mismatches are header-led leaves:
1242	                // their `TS2618`/`TS2619` (`Source has N element(s) but target
1243	                // requires/allows only M`) text carries no member name, so the
1244	                // union renderer supplies the `Type 'M' is not assignable to type
1245	                // 'T'.` member header (via `union_member_nested_needs_header`)
1246	                // before drilling the arity leaf — matching tsc:
1247	                //   Type '[2, 3] | [4]' is not assignable to type '[number]'.
1248	                //     Type '[2, 3]' is not assignable to type '[number]'.
1249	                //       Source has 2 element(s) but target allows only 1.

Good, confirmed low ids (6,7). Now let's edit the loop to sort members by TypeId ascending before iterating.

lines: +            // tsc's `eachTypeRelatedToType` walks the source union in ascending
+            // type-id order, not printer order. Intrinsic `undefined`/`null` are
+            // allocated first (low ids) but tsz's printer sorts them last for
+            // display, so without re-sorting here we'd elaborate a later,
+            // non-nullish member instead of the nullish one tsc reports.
// Defensive: avoid self-recursion on a degenerate union.

lines: // tsc's `eachTypeRelatedToType` walks the source union in ascending
-            // type-id order, not printer order. Intrinsic `undefined`/`null` are
-            // allocated first (low ids) but tsz's printer sorts them last for
-            // display, so without re-sorting here we'd elaborate a later,
-            // non-nullish member instead of the nullish one tsc reports.
+            // type-id order, where the intrinsic `undefined`/`null` types are
+            // allocated first (low ids). tsz's printer instead sorts nullish
+            // members last for display, so without restoring nullish-first
+            // priority here we'd elaborate a later, non-nullish member instead
+            // of the nullish one tsc reports. Non-nullish members keep their
+            // original (source/printer) relative order, since their type-id
+            // order does not reliably follow source order.
// Defensive: avoid self-recursion on a degenerate union.

content.text:
All 21 tests pass.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The fix targets one specific structural rule: tsc's `eachTypeRelatedToType` walks union members in ascending `TypeId` order, and intrinsics like `undefined`/`null` get allocated the lowest ids at startup — before any user type. tsz stores union members in *printer* order (nullish last, for readable display), so the elaboration loop was picking whichever member happened to be first in that display order, not the one tsc actually surfaces.

Rather than fully sorting by raw `TypeId` (which broke a different test — two fresh anonymous object-literal types don't reliably intern in source order relative to each other), the fix does a targeted stable partition: move `undefined`/`null` to the front, leave everyone else's relative order untouched. This is a case where "mirror tsc's real algorithm exactly" (full id-sort) was actually *wrong* for structural types, and a narrower, more conservative fix was the correct general solution.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

The failing test `union_member_selection_prioritizes_nullish_like_tsc` expected `undefined` to be elaborated in `number | undefined` assigned to `string`, but tsz was picking `number` instead because it iterated union members in printer order (nullish last) rather than tsc's type-id order (nullish first). Fixed in `crates/tsz-solver/src/relations/subtype/explain.rs` by reordering the member-elaboration loop to check nullish members (`undefined`/`null`) before the rest, preserving relative order otherwise. All 21 tests in the suite now pass.
